coredata.dc:title: Modulation of the binding ability to biomacromolecule, cytotoxicity and cellular imaging property for ionic liquid mediated carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Tingting
Wang, Xiaojuan
Hong, Xiaoqin
Xu, Wang
Shu, Yang
Wang, Jianhua
coredata.dc:description: For the preparation of carbon dots (CDs), a variety of carbon sources and synthetic protocols are available which endow CDs with variable and unpredictable properties. In the present study, three CDs were developed with ionic liquid 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide as the precursor through ethanol-thermal and hydrothermal strategies, termed as E-CDs and H-CDs, respectively. The features of these carbon dots, i.e., their physicochemical and optical properties, their interactions with bovine serum albumin (BSA) as well as their imaging capability were investigated with respect to the CDs prepared with microwave assisted approach (W-CDs). E-CDs and H-CDs were demonstrated to exhibit similar framework structures and optical properties, and they exhibited larger particle-sizes than that of W-CDs. In addition, the increase of ethanol-thermal and hydrothermal reaction time strengthened the quantum yields of the CDs and promoted their binding capability with BSA. E-CDs and H-CDs showed similar cytotoxicity on normal (LX-2) and cancer (SK-Hep-1) cells. We further found that these CDs may readily enter the cells within 5 min, while the fluorescence of hydrophilic E-CDs and H-CDs was very weak with respect to that of hydrophobic W-CDs in cell imaging. On the other hand, all the CDs exhibited little impact on the level of intracellular reactive oxygen species. The present study is conducive to guide the preparation of suitable carbon dots for different application scenarios.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Preparation protocols
Bovine serum albumin
Fluorescence quenching
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cytotoxicity assays, reactive oxygen species generation and cell imaging
BSA–CDs binding constants and thermodynamic parameters
Conformation changes of BSA after interacting with CDs
The binding sites of CDs to BSA
Cytotoxicity evaluation for CDs and their cellular imaging applications

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images and (B) particle-size distributions of the derived carbon dots (CDs) with ionic liquid 1-butly-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide as the precursor. (a) E-BCDs (ethanol-thermal strategy for 12 h), (b) H-BCDs1 (hydrothermal strategy for 12 h), (c) H-BCDs2 (hydrothermal strategy for 48 h), (d) W-BCDs (microwave-assisted approach).

Fluorescence (FL) spectra of (A) bovine serum albumin (BSA) in the presence of various concentrations of the derived carbon dots (CDs) with IL1-butly-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide as the precursor, (a) E-BCDs (ethanol-thermal strategy for 12 h), (b) H-BCDs1 (hydrothermal strategy for 12 h), (c) H-BCDs2 (hydrothermal strategy for 48 h), (d) W-BCDs (microwave-assisted approach), and (B) the derived CDs (100 μg/mL). (C) The Stern-Volmer curves of BSA fluorescence quenching by E-BCDs, H-BCDs1, H-BCDs2, and W-BCDs. Excitation wavelength at λex= 280 nm, CBSA= 10 μM, CCDs= 0, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500 μg/mL. 298 K, pH 7.4. The scanning speed for fluorescence recording was set at 1200 nm/min. Excitation and emission slit widths were set at 5 nm each with a voltage of 500 V.

The synchronous fluorescence spectra of BSA by interacting with different concentrations of various carbon dots (CDs) derived from ionic liquid 1-butly-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide. (A) Δλ= 15 nm, (B) Δλ= 60 nm. (a) E-BCDs (ethanol-thermal strategy for 12 h), (b) H-BCDs1 (hydrothermal strategy for 12 h), and (c) H-BCDs2 (hydrothermal strategy for 48 h). CBSA= 10 μM. At 298 K, pH 7.4.

The viabilities of SK-Hep-1 cells by treatment with different concentration of carbon dots (CDs). (a–f) CDs derived from ionic liquid 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide by microwave-assisted scheme, i.e., (a) W-ECDs (ethyl), (b) W-BCDs (butyl), (c) W-HCDs (hexyl), (d) W-OCDs (octyl), (e) W-DCDs (decyl), and (f) W-BOCDs (butyl, hydrophobic), (g–i) CDs derived from 1-butly-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide by different approaches, i.e., (g) E-BCDs (ethanol-thermal strategy for 12 h), (h) H-BCDs1 (hydrothermal strategy for 12 h), (i) H-BCDs2 (hydrothermal strategy for 48 h). The error bars indicate the standard deviations from three independent measurements.

Confocal fluorescence images of SK-Hep-1 cells co-cultured with 20 μg/mL CDs for 2 h. (a–f) CDs derived from ionic liquid 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide by microwave-assisted scheme, i.e., (a) W-ECDs (ethyl), (b) W-BCDs (butyl), (c) W-HCDs (hexyl), (d) W-OCDs (octyl), (e) W-DCDs (decyl), and (f) W-BOCDs (butyl, hydrophobic), (g–i) CDs derived from 1-butly-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide by different approaches, i.e., (g) E-BCDs (ethanol-thermal strategy for 12 h), (h) H-BCDs1 (hydrothermal strategy for 12 h), (i) H-BCDs2 (hydrothermal strategy for 48 h). Fluorescence channel, excitation/emission wavelength: 405/420–500 nm.

Illustration for the prepared methods of a series of CDs from 1-butly-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (W-BCDs, H-BCDs1, H-BCDs2 and W-BCDs) and their interaction with BSA and application in bioimaging.

head.ce:title: Modulation of the binding ability to biomacromolecule, cytotoxicity and cellular imaging property for ionic liquid mediated carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the preparation of carbon dots (CDs), a variety of carbon sources and synthetic protocols are available which endow CDs with variable and unpredictable properties. In the present study, three CDs were developed with ionic liquid 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide as the precursor through ethanol-thermal and hydrothermal strategies, termed as E-CDs and H-CDs, respectively. The features of these carbon dots, i.e., their physicochemical and optical properties, their interactions with bovine serum albumin (BSA) as well as their imaging capability were investigated with respect to the CDs prepared with microwave assisted approach (W-CDs). E-CDs and H-CDs were demonstrated to exhibit similar framework structures and optical properties, and they exhibited larger particle-sizes than that of W-CDs. In addition, the increase of ethanol-thermal and hydrothermal reaction time strengthened the quantum yields of the CDs and promoted their binding capability with BSA. E-CDs and H-CDs showed similar cytotoxicity on normal (LX-2) and cancer (SK-Hep-1) cells. We further found that these CDs may readily enter the cells within 5 min, while the fluorescence of hydrophilic E-CDs and H-CDs was very weak with respect to that of hydrophobic W-CDs in cell imaging. On the other hand, all the CDs exhibited little impact on the level of intracellular reactive oxygen species. The present study is conducive to guide the preparation of suitable carbon dots for different application scenarios.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a class of fluorescent nanomaterials have been explored in biological and medical applications [1–3] due to their excellent biocompatibility and high photostability. CDs are acknowledged as quasi-spherical carbonaceous nanoparticles with the size less than 10 nm. They are composed of carbon cores and surface structures. Carbon cores usually contain amorphous or nanocrystalline structure dominated by sp2 hybridized carbons [4], and are rarely diamond-like structure formed by sp3 carbon [5]. The surface structures are greatly varied by preparation schemes and precursors used, which further endow CDs exceptional properties in terms of fluorescence and cytotoxicity/biocompatibility.

A variety of approaches were reported for the preparation of CDs. By using different carbon sources, i.e., bulk materials or small molecules, the preparation schemes can be divided into two categories, i.e., top-down and bottom-up strategies [6]. The preparation may be accomplished by chemical oxidation, laser ablation, pyrolysis, hydrothermal/solvothermal/electrochemical/microwave treatments [7–16]. CDs prepared by chemical oxidation exhibited plenty oxygen-containing groups on their surface to facilitate bioimaging applications [4,7]. Their morphology/size and fluorescence may be readily adjusted by the laser power density, laser pulse width or wavelength [8,9]. In electrochemical processes, carbon electrodes and electrolytes may serve as carbon sources, enabling CDs with tunable fluorescence [10]. In pyrolysis treatment, the hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity and heteroatom-doping type can be manipulated using different surface passivators or appropriate precursors [11,12]. Microwave treatment facilitated extremely rapid preparation of CDs and their properties are heavily dependent on the choice of precursors [13,14]. Hydrothermal/solvothermal preparation endowed CDs more flexibility in morphology/size and optical performance by adjusting reaction conditions [7,15,16], which is critical for manipulating their applications in biomedicine and sensing.

Cytotoxicity/biocompatibility of CDs must be considered before bio-applications. Numerous cytotoxicity studies in vitro have shown that CDs exhibit very low toxicity and can be easily taken up by cells for imaging [17,18]. The cytotoxic effects of CDs, e.g., viability, mortality and proliferation, have been tested in different types of cell lines. The level of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) may reflect the cytotoxicity of CDs. CDs can induce ROS generation due to surface functionalization [19]. For in vivo studies, the interaction of CDs with protein should be taken into consideration. In biological fluid, CDs may combine with proteins to form a ‘protein corona’ [20], which could affect the biological response of CDs.

Ionic liquids (ILs) have been used to prepare and modify CDs as solvents, catalysts, passivators and modifiers [21,22]. We have fully discussed the mediation of physicochemical, fluorescence and biological features of CDs with ILs in a previous review [23]. In addition, the structure-activity relationship of CDs prepared with IL 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide via microwave-assisted protocol was exploited [24,25]. The hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity, surface structures, N-doping degree, quantum yield and cytotoxicity of CDs may be regulated by the alkyl side chain length in IL precursors. On the other hand, however, these properties may further be closely correlated with the adopted preparation strategies.

In the present work, IL 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide was used as precursor to prepare hydrophilic CDs by ethanol-thermal and hydrothermal schemes. The structure-activity relationship was investigated, in terms of morphology, cytotoxicity to different cell lines, their effect on intracellular ROS generation, and cell imaging ability. The results of this study may provide further in-depth information on the design and functionalization of CDs for specific purposes.
Details on chemicals and instrumentations were presented in Supporting Information (SI).

The microwave-assisted procedure for CDs preparation was given previously [24]. For ethanol-thermal preparation of E-CDs, 1.0 g of IL 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide was added into 5 mL of ethanol served as solvent. The mixture was transferred into 25 mL Teflon tank and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the supernatant was collected through centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and then dialyzed against deionized water through a dialysis bag (MWCO 100–500 Da) for 48 h. The CDs were collected by lyophilizing the dialyzed medium. For hydrothermal preparation of the H-CDs, the solvent ethanol was replaced with water and the above steps were followed, except for adopting different heating time of 12 h and 48 h. Finally, the solid CDs were dispersed in deionized water at a concentration of 8 mg/mL for further studies.

P-nitrophenyl acetate (PNPA) can be hydrolyzed to p-nitrophenol after interaction with BSA [26]. The esterase-like activity assay of BSA (10 μM) was explored by monitoring the absorbance of p-nitrophenol at 400 nm using Microplate reader. A molar extinction coefficient (ε = 17 700 M–1 cm–1) of p-nitrophenol was used for the calculations. CCDs= 100 μg/mL, CPNPA= 50 μM. The experiment was measured at 37 °C and tested three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assays, reactive oxygen species generation and cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Human liver cancer cells (SK-Hep-1) and human hepatic stellate cells (LX-2) were cultured in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% streptomycin and penicillin at 37 °C in an environment with 5% CO2.

A standard MTT assay was employed to examine the cytotoxicity of CDs. 100 μL of the above cell suspension (ca. 104 cells per well) was added into each well of a 96-well plate and pre-cultured for 24 h. The culture medium was then replaced with DMEM containing various concentrations of CDs (2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200 μg/mL) to culture another 20 h. Thereafter, 10 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well followed by incubation for 4 h. 100 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added into each well after removing the medium. The absorbance at 490 nm was measured by using a microplate reader after shaking for 10 min at room temperature.

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) analysis in SK-Hep-1 cells induced by CDs was conducted similar to that of MTT assay. In a 96-well plate, the pre-cultured SK-Hep-1 cells were treated with DMEM containing 10, 50,100, 200 μg/mL of CDs and incubated for 24 h. Then the DMEM was removed and replaced by PBS solution (100 μL per well) containing 10 μM of fluorescent ROS probe (General Oxidative Stress Indicator H2DCFDA), which was pre-dissolved in DMSO (10 mM) [19,27]. The plate was incubated for 30 min and the fluorescence was recorded using a microplate reader at λex/λem= 488/525 nm.

For assessing the cellular imaging capacity of CDs, SK-Hep-1 cell suspension (ca. 105 cells) was seeded into cell culture dishes and incubated for 12 h. Afterwards the culture medium was replaced with DMEM containing CDs (20 μg/mL). The cell imaging was recorded by a confocal fluorescence microscope with excitation/emission wavelength at 405/420–520 nm.

IL 1-butly-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (BmimDCN) was utilized as precursor to prepare CDs by different approaches. The obtained hydrophilic CDs were termed as E-BCDs (ethanol-thermal strategy for 12 h), H-BCDs1 (hydrothermal strategy for 12 h), and H-BCDs2 (hydrothermal strategy for 48 h), respectively ( Scheme 1). In parallel, CDs derived from 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide by microwave-assisted scheme were termed as W-ECDs (ethyl), W-BCDs (butyl), W-HCDs (hexyl), W-OCDs (octyl), W-DCDs (decyl), and W-BOCDs (butyl, hydrophobic) [24,25].

The morphology/size of CDs were characterized in transmission electron microscope (TEM) images ( Fig. 1). It was seen clearly that all CDs shown quasi-spherical shape, and the size distributions for E-BCDs, H-BCDs1, H-BCDs2 were 11.9 ± 1.8 nm, 9.0 ± 1.9 nm, and 9.6 ± 1.7 nm, respectively. The particle-sizes of CDs with BmimDCN as the precursor were slightly different by following the order of E-BCDs > H-BCDs2 > H-BCDs1, and their diameters were all larger than that of W-BCDs, i.e., 3.8 ± 0.8 nm. It is known that the size of CDs is closely related to the nucleation speed. Small particles are usually formed by rapid nucleation and the size distributions are relatively uniform. The growth of core was mainly determined by the surface state and the solubility of original materials [22,28]. In the present work, the CDs were derived with the same precursor IL, i.e., BmimDCN. The solvent and heating time in ethanol-thermal and hydrothermal reactions may lead to size variation of the CDs [28]. In microwave-assisted protocol, the internal energy was generated from the interior of the precursors, which led to the rapid heating of reactants [29]. This may probably explain the fact that W-CDs exhibited a narrower size distribution than those derived from ethanol-thermal/hydrothermal approaches.

Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the derived CDs and the precursor BmimDCN were illustrated in Fig. S1. It was seen that E-BCDs, H-BCDs1 and H-BCDs2 displayed similar absorptions, wherein the bands of CH3, CH2 and dicyandiamide groups at ca. 3150/3100 cm−1, ca. 2850/2930 cm−1 and 2100–2400 cm−1 were not identified with respect to that in BmimDCN. The adsorption bands at 3300–3500 cm−1 could be ascribed to stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H groups. The clear absorptions at ca. 1670, 1090 and 930 cm−1 were the vibrations of CC/CO, C–O–C and N–H bonds, respectively [28,30,31]. These results suggested that the surfaces of E-BCDs, H-BCDs1 and H-BCDs2 were rich in oxygenated and nitrogenous functional groups. In addition, the zeta potential of these CDs (1 mg/mL in aqueous medium) was measured. The values for E-BCDs, H-BCDs1, H-BCDs2 were − 14.0 ± 1.4 mV, − 29.8 ± 2.4 mV, and − 13.0 ± 0.8 mV, respectively. All the CDs were negatively charged, and especially for H-BCDs1.

The optical properties of the derived CDs were assessed by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. UV–vis spectra in Fig. S2a illustrated broad absorption bands within 200–240 nm for E-BCDs, H-BCDs1 and H-BCDs2, which was attributed to the π→π*transition of CC bond [32]. The excitation-wavelength dependent fluorescence behavior was illustrated in fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. S3). For E-BCDs, an emission maximum was observed at λex/λem= 360/430 nm. It was obvious that fluorescence maximum displayed blue-shift from 450 to 410 nm with excitation at 270–310 nm. On the contrast, however, red-shift from 410 to 450 nm was encountered at λex= 310–380 nm. For the case of H-BCDs1, an emission maximum was observed at λex/λem= 290/410 nm. A blue-shift from 420 to 400 nm was found at λex= 270–300 nm, while a red-shift from 400 to 440 nm was identified at λex= 300–380 nm. As for H-BCDs2, an emission maximum was recorded at λex/λem= 320/380 nm. A red-shift from 380 to 430 nm was observed at λex= 290–380 nm. Meanwhile, two emissions were observed with excitation at λex= 270–290 nm. One remained unchanged at 420 nm and the other exhibited blue-shift from ca. 385–375 nm. These observations suggested that there may be multi-emissive centers in the derived CDs. The fluorescence behavior of CDs is sensitive to their sizes, heteroatom-doping levels, edge configurations, surface defects and functional groups [33]. In addition, the possible mechanisms for the formation of CDs may be induced through pre-carbonization and post-polymerization processes with the variation of heating time [34], as clearly demonstrated by the cases of H-BCDs1 and H-BCDs2. All the above factors may cause different excitation-dependent emission behavior of the derived CDs.

The absolute fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of the derived CDs were exploited with excitation at λex= 330 nm for comparison with that of the W-BCDs. The QY values were 18.8 ± 0.9, 3.7 ± 1.6, 15.5 ± 1.0%, 12.0 ± 0.2% for E-BCDs, H-BCDs1, H-BCDs2, W-BCDs, respectively, illustrating a sequence of E-BCDs > H-BCDs2 > W-BCDs > H-BCDs1. These results indicated that at a same reaction time, the solvent exhibited obvious impact on the QY value, i.e., ethanol-thermal protocol generated a higher QY than hydrothermal process. In addition, the increase of heating time in hydrothermal process further improved the QY value.

The stability of fluorescence for the above derived CDs were evaluated in aqueous medium by varying the concentration of salt and pH value. Fig. S4 illustrated excellent fluorescence stability for E-BCDs, H-BCDs1 and H-BCDs2 even at a NaCl concentration of up to 500 mM. Fig. S5 further indicated that the fluorescence of these CDs is relatively stable within a range of pH 6–9. These observations suggested that the as-produced CDs were potentially suitable for bio-applications.

Changes of BSA optical properties after interaction with CDs have broad application prospects in treatment and diagnosis due to their multifunctional properties and superior biocompatibility [3]. Once CDs are employed for in vivo studies, the interaction of CDs with biomacromolecules should be carefully considered, which may affect their toxicity and metabolism. For this purpose, BSA as a model biomacromolecule was utilized for interacting with the derived CDs. UV–vis absorption spectrum of BSA showed two typical absorption bands (Fig. S2a). The absorption at 225 nm is assigned to π–π*transition from CO polypeptide skeleton, while the other at 280 nm was attributed to n–π*transition of aromatic amino acids, e.g., tryptophan (Trp), tyrosine (Tyr) and phenylalanine (Phe) [35]. The interaction of CDs with BSA may cause changes on the UV–vis absorption spectrum of BSA, as clearly illustrated in Fig. S2b for the variation of absorption of BSA at 280 nm. This implied that the change of the microenvironment around the amino acid residues in BSA. Considering that the dynamic quenching mechanism only poses effect on the emission of the fluorophore, while hardly causes influence on its absorption [36]. Thus, it can be preliminarily judged from the observations herein that fluorescence of BSA may be statically quenched by the derived CDs.

BSA can emit bright fluorescence with a maximum excitation wavelength at λex= 280 nm ascribing to aromatic amino residues, e.g., tryptophan (Trp), tyrosine (Tyr) and phenylalanine [37,38]. Fig. 2A illustrated decrement on the fluorescence intensity of BSA by increasing the concentrations of E-BCDs, H-BCDs1, H-BCDs2, and W-BCDs. This clearly suggested the existence of interactions between BSA and the CDs. The results indicated that fluorescence quenching capability of E-BCDs and H-BCDs2 seemed to be more obvious than that of H-BCDs1. In addition, the fluorescence quenching capability of W-BCDs was more obvious with respect those derived by ethanol-thermal and hydrothermal approaches, as the fluorescence of BSA can be significantly quenched at a concentration of 4–20 μg/mL W-BCDs [24]. In practical applications, it is highly necessary to evaluate the potential interfering effects of the derived CDs with various preparation strategies on the fluorescence of BSA. The results in Fig. 2B clearly demonstrated that the solutions of 100 μg/mL CDs gave rise to very limited fluorescence with excitation at λex= 280 nm, which was safely negligible with respect to fluorescence of BSA at the concentration of 10 μM, i.e., the native fluorescence of CDs caused no interference on that of BSA.

The static and dynamic quenching of fluorescence may be distinguishable via temperature-dependent behaviors [37,38]. In static mechanism, the quenching efficiency decreases with the increase of temperature and ground-state complexes could be generated between the fluorophore and the quencher. In dynamic mechanism, the quenching efficiency increases as the increase of temperature, and collisions may occur between the fluorophore and the quencher. In the following Stern-Volmer (SV) equation [39], F0 and F are fluorescence of BSA in the absence and presence of CDs. [Q] represents the concentration of quencher (CDs), KSV is the SV quenching constant, Kq is the bimolecular quenching constant. τ0 is the average lifetime of biomacromolecule without quencher (CDs).
(1)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
Q
(2)
K
SV
=
K
q
τ
0

The SV plots exhibited different trends for E-BCDs, H-BCDs1, H-BCDs2, and W-BCDs by increasing their concentrations at 298 K (Fig. 2C). For further studies, the concentrations of E-BCDs and H-BCDs2 were chosen at 20–100 μg/mL, while that for H-BCDs1 was set at 100–500 μg/mL. The linear correlations were obtained between fluorescence ratio and CDs concentration at 298, 304, and 310 K (Fig. S6). The values of KSV and Kq for CD–BSA systems were listed in Table S1. At 298 K, KSV values followed a decrement sequence of E-BCDs (4.83 ×103 L/mol) > H-BCDs2 (3.05 ×103 L/mol) > H-BCDs1 (0.674 ×103 L/mol). These KSV values were smaller by one order of magnitude with respect to that of W-BCDs, i.e., 7.37 × 104 L/mol. These observations demonstrated that the interaction and binding capability of the CDs with BSA was associated with the preparation strategies, by following an order of microwave, ethanol-thermal and hydrothermal protocols. Besides, the results in Table S1 further indicated that a longer reaction time also strength the binding between the derived CDs and BSA as illustrated by H-BCDs1 and H-BCDs2. In addition, KSV values for the three kinds of CDs decreased with the increase of temperature, which indicated that static quenching mechanism between CDs and BSA may dominate the quenching process. Generally, the static quenching may be inferred by Kq values when they are bigger than the maximum bimolecular quenching constant of the quenchers for biomacromolecules, i.e., 2 × 1010 L/(mol s) [40]. As can be seen from Table S1, Kq values for all the CDs were more than 2 × 1010 L/(mol s). Thus, it is safely concluded that the fluorescence of BSA was mostly quenched by CDs through static quenching. Therefore, CD–BSA complex may be formed between CDs and BSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BSA–CDs binding constants and thermodynamic parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
The binding constant may reflect the binding affinity between CDs and BSA, and it is calculated by the following equation with Ka as the binding constant and n as the average number of binding sites [41].
(3)
lg
F
0
-F
F
=
lg
K
a
+ nlg
Q

The binding constants and the average number of binding sites of CD–BSA systems were summarized in Table S2. The binding constants of 589, 1870, 728 L/mol for E-BCDs, H-BCDs1, H-BCDs2 at 298 K were smaller than W-BCDs at the level of 104 L/mol. This result indicated that the W-BCDs had stronger binding capability with BSA. The average number of binding site n was derived to be approximately 1.0, i.e., 0.777–1.60 at 298, 303 and 310 K. This indicated that the CDs had one binding site on BSA, i.e., CD–BSA complex was formed with a molar ratio of 1:1.

In combination of the quencher with the biomolecules, four kinds of binding forces are generally involved, i.e., van der Waals, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions. The binding forces could be inferred by thermodynamic parameters, i.e., the changes of entropy (ΔS), enthalpy (ΔH), and Gibbs free energy (ΔG). These values for E-BCDs, H-BCDs1, H-BCDs2 and W-BCDs were derived based on Van’t Hoff equation [42], as summarized in Table S2. The positive values for ΔH and ΔS indicated that the binding of CDs to BSA was mainly entropy-driven, with hydrophobic interactions playing a major role in the interaction. The negative values for ΔG in all the CD–BSA systems demonstrated that the binding process of CDs to BSA was spontaneous. Besides, the weakness of electrostatic interaction was further confirmed by the increase of ionic strength in CD–BSA systems. As seen in Fig. S7, the normalized intensity of all CD–BSA systems displayed no obvious change with increasing concentration of NaCl (0–0.5 mol/L), which implied that the electrostatic interaction is negligible between all the CDs and BSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conformation changes of BSA after interacting with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synchronous fluorescence spectra were recorded for further exploitation of the changes in the microenvironment around the fluorescent amino acid residues. The shift in the maximum emission wavelength is adopted to assess the changes of amino acid residues in BSA. In the synchronous fluorescence spectra of BSA, wavelength interval (Δλ) with 15 and 60 nm may provide varieties of Tyr and Trp residues [43]. Fig. 3 illustrated the decrease for fluorescence intensity at maximum emission for the cases of E-BCDs, H-BCDs1, H-BCDs2. This indicated the change of polarity around Tyr and Trp residues, which was consistent with the observations in fluorescence quenching. The maximum emission wavelength of Δλ= 15 nm remained virtually unchanged, while that at Δλ= 60 nm exhibited a slight red-shift. The observations herein revealed that CDs cause more obvious effect on the polarity near the Trp residue in BSA. It was thus reasonable to assume that the binding site of CDs was close to this region.

Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were measured to study the changes of secondary structure in BSA induced by the interaction with CDs. The two negative bands at 209 and 222 nm are attributed to the characteristic absorptions of the α-helical structure and the n-π*transition of BSA peptide sequence [44]. As illustrated in Fig. S8, there were two negative bands at 211 and 222 nm in the CD spectra of BSA, while no absorptions were identified in the spectra of bare CDs. For the cases of E-BCDs and H-BCDs2, they caused virtually no change on the CD spectra of BSA. But for H-BCDs1, the negative band at 211 nm increased obviously with the increment of CDs concentrations. This observation implied that E-BCDs and H-BCDs2 exhibited very little impact on the secondary structure of BSA, while H-BCDs1 induced the decrease of α-helical structure in BSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The binding sites of CDs to BSA
ce:section.ce:para:
The competitive displacement experiments were exploited to infer the binding site of CDs to BSA. Two site probes were used for this purpose, i.e., phenylbutazone (site I) and ibuprofen (site II) [45]. The displacement of the site probes to the sites occupied by CDs results in significant changes in the BSA fluorescence spectrum. The calculation formula of the probe displacement is given in the following [46], with F1, F2 as the fluorescence of BSA in CD–BSA systems in the absence and presence of the site probes.
(4)
Probe displacement (%) =
F
2
/
F
1
× 100 %

As displayed in Fig. S9, the addition of site probe phenylbutazone exhibited more obvious influence on the fluorescence of BSA in the CD–BSA complex with respect to that of ibuprofen. It illustrated that phenylbutazone substituted CDs from the binding site of BSA, while the effect of ibuprofen on CD–BSA interaction was not significant. Therefore, it was conclusive that CDs mainly bind within the site I of BSA, which was close to the Trp-213 residue. This result was well consistent with that observed in the synchronous fluorescence.

In terms of biological applications, the effect of CDs on BSA activity is essential. The activity of BSA was evaluated by monitoring the esterase-like activity of BSA [26]. The substrate PNPA was selected to explore the catalytic activity of BSA by measuring the absorbance at 400 nm of the p-nitrophenol hydrolysate. The kinetic profiles for the effect of the CDs, i.e., E-BCDs, H-BCDs1, H-BCDs2 and W-BCDs, on the activity of BSA were illustrated in Fig. S10a. And the relative activity of BSA induced by CDs are shown in Fig. S10b. One unit of activity stands for the required amount of BSA to release 1 μM of p-nitrophenol from PNPA per minute at 37 °C. The relative activity of BSA induced by E-BCDs, H-BCDs1, H-BCDs2 and W-BCDs was 103 ± 10.8%, 104 ± 1.2%, 96.5 ± 0.4%, 102 ± 4.2%, respectively. The esterase-like activity of BSA exhibited no obvious variation, implying that CDs obtained affect the activity of BSA slightly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation for CDs and their cellular imaging applications

ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of CDs are most important for their applications in biosensing, bioimaging, anticancer drug delivery, and other biological studies [3,47]. The cytotoxicity of CDs was investigated by standard MTT assays. Cancer cells SK-Hep-1 and normal cells LX-2 were chosen to evaluate the various CDs derived by ethanol-thermal, hydrothermal and microwave-assisted strategies. As illustrated in Fig. 4, the hydrophilic CDs prepared by different strategies with the same IL precursor BmimDCN, i.e., W-BCDs, E-BCDs, H-BCDs1and H-BCDs2, exhibited similar SK-Hep-1 cell viabilities. The survival rate of SK-Hep-1 cells remained > 80% at a concentration level of 200 μg/mL. This well demonstrated the suitability of these CDs for potential biological purposes/applications. On the other hand, the hydrophobic CDs (W-BOCDs) was more cytotoxic than the hydrophilic counterparts. The reason for this result may be that W-BOCDs enhance their cellular uptake via hydrophobic interactions, and the membrane damage to increase cytotoxicity [48]. In addition, the CDs displayed a stronger SK-Hep-1 cytotoxicity with the increase of the alkyl side chain length in their precursor ILs, which was consistent with MCF-7 cytotoxicity in our previous study [24]. For normal cells LX-2 (Fig. S11), the cytotoxicity of these CDs was similar to that for SK-Hep-1 cells. It may be that the longer the alkyl chain decorated on CDs, the easier it is to embed into the cell membrane, thereby destroying the cells [49]. Hence, the cytotoxicity of CDs prepared from ILs containing increasing alkyl side chain gradually increased.

Oxidative stress is the main cytotoxic mechanism triggered by nano materials [19,50], and various cell lines show different antioxidant capacities. The formation of high level of ROS will lead to oxidative damage and death of cells [51]. The increase of alkyl side chain length in precursor ILs led to stronger cytotoxicity of the obtained CDs, which may be attributed to the production of intracellular ROS [52]. Therefore, ROS production in SK-Hep-1 cells was assessed after incubating with the CDs for 24 h. As demonstrated in Fig. S12, the variation of CDs concentration within 10–200 μg/mL led to limited change of ROS levels in SK-Hep-1 cells. The observation well demonstrated that the generation of intracellular ROS by the CDs made virtually no contribution to their cytotoxicity.

CDs have been applied in bioimaging, like organelle-targeting, living body imaging, and so on [53–55]. Cell imaging capability was evaluated for the CDs derived by different strategies. Confocal fluorescence images ( Fig. 5) illustrated that only W-BOCDs emit bright green fluorescence in the SK-Hep-1 cells after incubation for 2 h, and only very weak fluorescence was recorded for the other hydrophilic CDs, i.e., W-ECDs, W-BCDs, W-HCDs, W-OCDs, W-DCDs, E-BCDs, H-BCDs1 and H-BCDs2. This was probably because the hydrophobicity of W-BOCDs made itself enter cells more easily than other hydrophilic CDs through hydrophobic interactions [48]. The real-time fluorescence of the CDs uptake by SK-Hep-1 cells was monitored from 5 min to 90 min as illustrated in Fig. S13. Although the fluorescence of these hydrophilic CDs was quite weak, their uptake into the cells can still be clearly identified within 5 min. The fluorescence intensity of hydrophobic CDs, i.e., W-BOCDs, increased with time up to 40 min. The observations herein demonstrated that the hydrophilic CDs may be more difficult to enter the cell interior than the hydrophobic CDs [56]. In this respect, the hydrophobic CDs derived with IL as the precursor may have promising potential biological applications.

Hydrophilic carbon dots were prepared by ethanol-thermal and hydrothermal approaches with ionic liquid 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide as the precursor. Their properties, i.e., optical feature, cytotoxicity, interactions with BSA, as well as cell imaging capability, were thoroughly exploited and compared with the CDs derived by microwave assisted protocol with IL 1-alkyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (alkyl including ethyl, butyl, hexyl, octyl, decyl) as the precursor. The ethanol-thermally and hydrothermally prepared CDs exhibited similar structures and excitation-dependent fluorescence emission features. It revealed that the wide range properties of CDs may be well regulated by varying the preparation protocols, the type of solvents and the reaction conditions, including but not limited to the reaction time and temperature. The present work provides a potential avenue for the design and regulation of ionic liquid mediated carbon dots for various purpose in biological studies.

Tingting Guo: Investigation, Data curation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Xiaojuan Wang: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoqin Hong: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Wang Xu: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Yang Shu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jianhua Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (Nos. 21974018, 22074011 and 22174013).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2022.112552.
